profile.name: Nick Cook
roles: Author - The Hunt for Zero Point (2001/2002)
Director, Bigelow Institute for Consciousness Studies (BICS)
profile.summary: British aviation journalist who served as Aviation Editor of Jane's Defence Weekly for nearly 15 years, winning four Royal Aeronautical Society journalism awards. His 2001 book 'The Hunt for Zero Point' documented a decade-long investigation into classified US antigravity research programs, drawing connections between post-war Operation Paperclip Nazi science absorption, T. Townsend Brown's electrogravitics experiments, and the alleged black program development of inertial mass reduction technology. His mainstream defense credentials distinguish him from fringe UAP authors, though critics note his conclusions outrun his evidence. He later became a director of Robert Bigelow's Institute for Consciousness Studies.
education: BA (Hons), Arabic and Islamic Studies, University of Exeter (1982)
early_career: Technology reporter for Interavia and International Defense Review in the mid-1980s
Joined Jane's Defence Weekly as Aviation Editor in 1987; covered global aerospace and defense technology for nearly 15 years
Gained access to classified US and former Soviet defense facilities in reporting capacity
Won four Aerospace Journalist of the Year Awards from the Royal Aeronautical Society (Defence, Business, Technology, and Propulsion categories)
Contributed to Financial Times and Wall Street Journal; called upon as expert analyst during 1991 and 2003 Iraq conflicts

key_events.event: Joined Jane's Defence Weekly as Aviation Editor. Over the next 14 years covered global aerospace and defense technology, broke stories on classified Russian and US programs, and gained access to top-secret facilities on both sides of the former Iron Curtain.

Stumbled upon a declassified 1950s technical report on electrogravitics while working at Jane's. The document described serious research by major US aerospace contractors into gravity control in the mid-1950s, before the field abruptly vanished from public record around 1957-1958. This discovery launched his decade-long investigation that became The Hunt for Zero Point.

Provided expert commentary and analysis during the Gulf War as a recognised defense and aviation authority, cementing his mainstream credentials alongside his private research into classified propulsion programs.

Wrote and presented 'Billion Dollar Secret' for the Discovery Channel - his first documentary, investigating classified US military spending and experimental aircraft as possible explanations for UAP sightings. IMDB rating: 6.9.

Published 'The Hunt for Zero Point: Inside the Classified World of Antigravity Technology' (Century Random House, UK). US edition followed from Broadway Books in 2002. The book reached No. 1 in Amazon's non-fiction chart on UK publication.

US hardcover edition of The Hunt for Zero Point published by Broadway Books. Documents: 1950s electrogravitics research by major US aerospace contractors; T. Townsend Brown's suppressed high-voltage disc experiments; Nazi SS General Hans Kammler's advanced weapons program and the alleged Die Glocke device; and potential connections to post-war black programs and the B-2 bomber design. Key anonymous source 'Marckus' and physicist Hal Puthoff feature as primary references.

Wrote and presented 'UFOs: The Secret Evidence' for History Channel (US title: 'An Alien History of Planet Earth'). Investigates UAP sightings from the 1940s through 1990s in the context of classified military programs. IMDB rating: 7.5. Also appeared in a Discovery Channel 'Unsolved History' episode on Area 51.

Co-wrote 'Barefoot Soldier: A Story of Extreme Valour' with Johnson Beharry VC, the British soldier awarded the Victoria Cross for Iraq service. The book became a mainstream bestseller, entirely separate from his UAP research and demonstrating his range as a writer beyond the defence and anomalies space.

Founded Dynamixx, a defence-to-civilian technology consultancy focused on transitioning aerospace and defense technologies to broader commercial applications. Marked his formal shift away from Jane's-style investigative journalism toward technology strategy work.

Launched NickCook.Works, a corporate narrative strategy consultancy. Published 'The Grid' - a thriller about surveillance and perception manipulation, named a London Times Book of the Year. Research interests shifted increasingly toward consciousness studies and the intersection of UAP phenomena with non-material experience.

Submitted an entry to Robert Bigelow's essay competition on evidence for survival of human consciousness after physical death, winning a $20,000 prize. Bigelow subsequently invited Cook to become a director of the Bigelow Institute for Consciousness Studies (BICS), establishing a formal institutional connection to the primary private funder of UAP and consciousness research in the United States.

credibility.note: Cook occupies a genuinely unusual position in UAP discourse. His nearly 15 years as aviation editor of Jane's Defence Weekly, four Royal Aeronautical Society journalism awards, and mainstream bestseller 'Barefoot Soldier' place him in a different category from fringe UAP writers. His investigative methodology follows archival breadcrumbs from real declassified documents and interviews named and anonymous sources - credible as journalism even where conclusions are disputed. Critical weaknesses include heavy reliance on anonymous source 'Marckus', the unverifiable Die Glocke narrative sourced through Witkowski, and conclusions that exceed the evidence. His later revision of the Die Glocke claims is a mark of intellectual honesty but underscores the speculative nature of that section.
supporting: Nearly 15 years as Aviation Editor of Jane's Defence Weekly, the world's most authoritative open-source military technology reference
Four Aerospace Journalist of the Year Awards from the Royal Aeronautical Society in Defence, Business, Technology, and Propulsion categories
Demonstrated mainstream credibility via 'Barefoot Soldier' (2006) - a bestselling mainstream biography entirely outside UAP subject matter

Investigation grounded in a real, declassified 1950s technical report showing major US aerospace contractors genuinely funded electrogravitics research before its abrupt disappearance from public record
T. Townsend Brown's experiments are independently documented in contemporaneous aviation trade press from the 1950s
Hal Puthoff is a named, credentialed source rather than an anonymous contact
Book reached No. 1 on Amazon's UK non-fiction chart on publication, indicating serious mainstream readership beyond the UAP community
Later publicly revised his Die Glocke conclusions - showing willingness to update claims based on new assessment rather than defending a fixed narrative
contradicting: Primary human source 'Marckus' is anonymous and cannot be independently verified or cross-checked
Die Glocke narrative sourced almost entirely through Igor Witkowski's unverifiable claims; Cook himself later revised his view that it was antigravity-related, suggesting it was more likely nuclear
Mainstream reviews (Salon, The Skeptic) found conclusions significantly outrun the evidence presented
No declassified document or confirmed government source has corroborated the existence of a classified antigravity program of the kind Cook describes
Cook's degree is in Arabic and Islamic Studies - he relies on others to evaluate all technical and physics claims
The electrogravitics research disappearance from public record could reflect scientific abandonment due to poor results rather than classification
No independent journalist or government inquiry has confirmed any core classified program claim since the book's 2001 publication

associated_people.name: Dr. Hal Puthoff
associated_people.role: Theoretical physicist, Institute for Advanced Studies in Austin
associated_people.relationship: Puthoff is Cook's primary named expert source in The Hunt for Zero Point, providing the physics framework for zero-point energy and inertial mass reduction theory. Cook interviewed Puthoff directly and Puthoff's theoretical work forms the scientific scaffolding for Cook's classified antigravity program thesis.

associated_people.name: Robert Bigelow
associated_people.relationship: Bigelow awarded Cook a $20,000 prize in his consciousness studies essay competition and subsequently invited Cook to become a director of the Bigelow Institute for Consciousness Studies, establishing a formal institutional relationship between the two.

associated_people.name: Bob Lazar
associated_people.relationship: Lazar's claimed S-4 work on element 115 and gravity wave propulsion overlaps conceptually with the classified antigravity program thesis Cook documents. Both figures circulate in the same discourse space around suppressed advanced propulsion technology.

disclosures.title: Billion Dollar Secret
disclosures.notes: Written and presented by Cook. Investigates classified US military spending and experimental aircraft as possible explanations for UAP sightings. IMDB rating: 6.9.

disclosures.title: The Hunt for Zero Point: Inside the Classified World of Antigravity Technology
disclosures.notes: Primary written disclosure. Ten-year investigation into classified antigravity programs, electrogravitics, T. Townsend Brown, and the Nazi Bell (Die Glocke). No. 1 Amazon UK non-fiction on publication. US ISBN: 978-0-7679-0627-2.

disclosures.title: UFOs: The Secret Evidence
disclosures.outlet: History Channel (US: An Alien History of Planet Earth)
disclosures.notes: Written and presented by Cook. Investigates UAP sightings from the 1940s through 1990s in the context of classified military programs. IMDB rating: 7.5.

career_connections.relationship: Puthoff's theoretical work on zero-point energy and inertial mass reduction became the physics foundation of Cook's investigative thesis. Cook interviewed Puthoff as a named source for The Hunt for Zero Point, with Puthoff's Institute for Advanced Studies in Austin explicitly cited as providing the scientific framework for why classified antigravity programs would be worth concealing.

Lazar's claimed S-4 work on element 115 and gravity wave propulsion overlaps conceptually with the classified antigravity program thesis Cook documents in The Hunt for Zero Point. Cook's Jane's credentials provided the investigative legitimacy framework in which Lazar's claims were often re-examined by mainstream audiences.

Bigelow awarded Cook a $20,000 prize in his consciousness research essay competition and invited Cook to the board of the Bigelow Institute for Consciousness Studies. This formal institutional connection brought Cook into direct collaboration with the primary private funder of UAP and consciousness research in the United States.

sources.title: The Hunt for Zero Point - Nick Cook (Broadway Books, 2002)
sources.notes: Primary source - Cook's full account of his antigravity investigation. US ISBN: 978-0-7679-0627-2.
